coredata.dc:title: Exploring graphene quantum dots@TiO2 rutile (011) interface for visible-driven hydrogen production in photoelectrochemical cell: Density functional theory and experimental study
coredata.dc:creator: Ullah, Farman
Bashiri, Robabeh
Muti Mohamed, Norani
Zaleska-Medynska, Adriana
Kait, Chong Fai
Ghani, Usman
Shahid, Muhammad Umair
Saheed, Mohamed Shuaib Mohamed
coredata.dc:description: Insufficient knowledge on multiple processes at the interface of different phases of one-dimensional titanium dioxide (1D TiO2) photoanodes hinders the efficiency of solar-powered hydrogen production in a photoelectrochemical cell. Density functional theory (DFT) and experimental studies were performed to identify the interface characteristics of the heterojunction formation between 1D TiO2 (011) surface and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for the enhancement of photocatalytic hydrogen production. The interfacial electronic structure, charge transfer, and optical characteristics of the GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface were simulated and experimentally validated using Hubbard modified generalised gradient approximation (GGA + U). Both theoretical and experimental results confirmed the extension of optical absorption into the visible range and frequent charge transfer from GQD to the TiO2 rutile (011) surface, facilitating electron-hole separation and reducing charge recombination rate. Furthermore, the formation of highly crystalline TiO2 rutile (011) nanorods with uniform distribution of GQDs was validated through X-ray diffraction (XRD) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) results. The hydrogen production rate over GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) photoanode was 31063 µmol g−1 h−1, nearly five times more efficient than the pristine TiO2 rutile (011). Also, a mechanism for photogenerated electron transfer and energy-band-matching at the hybrid interface was proposed.

The XRD patterns of (a) GQD, (b) FTO, (c) TiO2, and (d) GQD@TiO2.

(a) HRTEM image of the GQDs, (b) size distribution of the GQDs, (c and d) TEM and HRTEM images of TiO2, (e) TEM and (f and g) HRTEM images of GQD@TiO2, and (h) SAED pattern of GQD@TiO2 nanorods at scale 5 (1/nm).

EIS data for charge transfer characteristics at electrode/electrolyte interface.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Insufficient knowledge on multiple processes at the interface of different phases of one-dimensional titanium dioxide (1D TiO2) photoanodes hinders the efficiency of solar-powered hydrogen production in a photoelectrochemical cell. Density functional theory (DFT) and experimental studies were performed to identify the interface characteristics of the heterojunction formation between 1D TiO2 (011) surface and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) for the enhancement of photocatalytic hydrogen production. The interfacial electronic structure, charge transfer, and optical characteristics of the GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface were simulated and experimentally validated using Hubbard modified generalised gradient approximation (GGA + U). Both theoretical and experimental results confirmed the extension of optical absorption into the visible range and frequent charge transfer from GQD to the TiO2 rutile (011) surface, facilitating electron-hole separation and reducing charge recombination rate. Furthermore, the formation of highly crystalline TiO2 rutile (011) nanorods with uniform distribution of GQDs was validated through X-ray diffraction (XRD) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) results. The hydrogen production rate over GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) photoanode was 31063 µmol g−1 h−1, nearly five times more efficient than the pristine TiO2 rutile (011). Also, a mechanism for photogenerated electron transfer and energy-band-matching at the hybrid interface was proposed.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the increasing global demand for energy and global warming, researchers must devise a method for generating and utilising clean and green sustainable energy fuels [1,2]. Hydrogen production via photocatalytic water splitting is one of the best replacements for these resources as feedstocks are abundant and the combustion product is only water [3,4]. Exploring a highly efficient semiconductor photocatalyst is therefore of great importance. Hence, researchers focussed on TiO2 due to its excellent physical and chemical stability, favourable band-edge positions, nontoxicity, low cost, high photo-corrosion resistance, and abundance [5]. 1D TiO2 has always been preferable over nanoparticles or bulk materials to fabricate efficient photocatalyst. It can perform fast, long-distance electron transfer and improve absorption and light scattering, originating from the high length-to-diameter ratio [6]. However, photocatalytic activity is restricted due to a broad bandgap and the rapid recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [7-9].

Therefore, multi-disciplinary approaches have been investigated, including metals, non-metals, and surface modifications by depositing other low bandgap metal oxides to overcome these challenges [10-15]. Surface modification of TiO2 with quantum dots (QDs) is a promising approach due to their unique properties such as size/component tunable bandgaps, broad absorption spectra, high extinction coefficients, and the possibility of multiple exciton generation makes them ideal building blocks for numerous optoelectronic devices like QDs sensitised solar cells and solar-driven photoelectrochemical [16,17]. GQDs have received much attention as a new class of QD materials that can modify semiconductor-based photocatalysts with enhanced photocatalytic and photoelectrochemical properties due to their intriguing optical and electrical properties and high resistance to harsh chemical attacks [18-21].

For instance, Min et al. [22] reported that the decoration of GQD on the surface of commercial TiO2 (P25) photocatalyst could effectively enhance the photocatalytic activity of hydrogen evolution rate from 0.3 to 2.2 µmol h−1 at optimal GQDs content than pristine TiO2 in the presence of 10% vol% ethanol in the batch reactor system. Meng et al. [23] investigated the effect of GQDs anchored on the surface of CdS@TiO2 nanotubes-array (NA) for photocatalytic hydrogen production in the presence of Na2S–Na2SO3 sacrificial agent. They reported a significantly higher hydrogen production rate of 18 µmol h−1 when compared to 10 and 13 µmol h−1 for TiO2 and CdS@TiO2 NAs, respectively. Ghosh et al. [24] studied the effect of various GQDs@TiO2 morphologies on photocatalytic hydrogen production in the presence of glycerol. They found that P25 TiO2-based nanocomposite produced the most hydrogen (29548 µmol g−1h−1), nearly 14 times more efficient than pristine P25 TiO2. Furthermore, Heo et al. [25] and Xu et al. [26] reported that the anchored GQD on the surface of TiO2 generated more hydrogen and had a higher PEC current than pristine TiO2.

These studies are still in the early stages of development, far from being practical, and mainly concentrated on experimental aspects, including different methods for synthesising GQDs and their decoration on different morphologies of TiO2. However, theoretical calculations are even less frequently reported. The lack of theoretical studies on modelling the electronic structure and charge transfer properties appears to be one of the main barriers to developing this hybrid photocatalyst [27-29]. Thus, the combination of experimental strategy and computational modelling is vital for understanding the interfacial electronic structure and atomic-scale photo-activity of GQD modified TiO2 hybrid photoelectrodes and improving their properties for efficient photocatalytic hydrogen production [30].

Furthermore, the surface orientations affect the material's photo-reactivity, as crystal faces have a pre-eminent role in nanomaterials. A large fraction of the atoms in nanomaterials exist at surface sites; hence, the growth of a particular crystal face can significantly affect the material's reactivity [31]. Among the three different phases of TiO2, rutile is the most stable and abundant crystal phase. Primarily, the TiO2 rutile (110) surface exhibits excellent stability, and many structural works have been carried out mainly on the (110) surface, as the (110) surface is relatively easy to prepare than the other TiO2 rutile surfaces. Recent developments indicate that the TiO2 rutile (011) surface is the second most stable facet and is more reactive toward photocatalyzed oxidation reactions than the TiO2 rutile (110) and other low Miller index surfaces [31,32]. Therefore, after a thorough literature review, these shortcomings provide a solid motivation to combine computational and experimental investigation for the catalytically active and less investigated TiO2 rutile (011) modified with GQDs as photoanode for solar hydrogen production in PEC cells.

To the best of our knowledge, this is the first time a comprehensive explanation of the electronic structure, optical properties, and charge transfer characteristics of the GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) surface has been provided. Understanding electron transfer on the photocatalyst surface provides an impetus for designing an efficient hybrid photoelectrode for a practical approach. Therefore, in this study, an in-depth discussion is provided on the electronic structure, optical properties, and charge transfer characteristics of TiO2 rutile (011) and GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) surface photocatalysts using first-principles calculations based on Hubbard's modified density functional theory (DFT + U) method. The DFT outcomes were evaluated by synthesising and investigating the physicochemical properties of TiO2 rutile (011) and GQD@TiO2 rutile (011). Finally, the photoelectrochemical properties and photocatalytic hydrogen production performance of the synthesised photocatalysts were investigated in the PEC cell. Both theoretical and experimental results showed that the proposed GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) heterostructure photocatalyst had enhanced photocatalytic performance due to improved visible light absorption and a significant number of charges migrated from GQD to the TiO2 rutile (011) surface.

The DFT calculations in this study were performed using the well-tested Cambridge sequential total energy package (CASTEP) module of the Materials Studio program. The CASTEP code practised plane-wave basis sets with a pseudopotential approach to treat the interaction of valence electrons with ionic cores [31]. The norm-conserving ultrasoft pseudopotential approach was adopted with periodic boundary conditions in three dimensions (3D). A Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof (PBE) exchange–correlation DFT functional with GGA was implemented [32]. The geometric structure of the bulk TiO2 rutile unit cell with GGA + PBE exchange correlational functional was optimised by allowing all the bulk atoms to relax during simulations. It was followed by building TiO2 rutile (011) surface from the optimised bulk TiO2 unit cell using surface slab model with Broyden-Fletcher-Goldfarb-Shanno (BFGS) algorithm to reconstruct the TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface model. The structure of the bulk TiO2 rutile and the TiO2 rutile (011) − 2 × 1 surface were optimised by setting the most significant upper limit of the residual force between atoms to allow a value of 0.02 eV/Å.

It should be noted that only the positions of the atoms were allowed to relax, while the volume and shape of the cells remained constant. The convergence criteria for the energy values were chosen below the 2 × 10-5 eV/atom and the cut-off energy value of 400 eV to expand the plane-wave basis set of Kohn-Sham wave functions. The favourable Monkhorst-Pack within the first Brillouin zone was used for k-point integration with specified (4 × 4 × 4, 4 × 8 × 1, and 4 × 4 × 1) Monckhorst-Pack grids used for bulk TiO2 rutile, TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1, and GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface model, respectively. For self-consistent field (SCF) iterations, the convergence threshold was set at 2 × 10-6 eV/atom, with an atomic displacement of less than 0.002 Å and a stress limitation of below 0.1 GPa.

TiO2 is a “strongly correlated” system with highly localised Ti3d states [33]. The conduction band (CB) is primarily composed of these localised 3d states. Therefore, standard DFT calculations generally flop to define the correct bandgap energy (Eg) for a strongly localised d-electrons system. The concept of Hubbard's modified (DFT + U) method was introduced for strongly correlated materials to consider the on-site Coulomb interaction caused by the repulsive force between two electrons of the same d orbitals. Extensive DFT + U calculations were performed to replicate the correct Eg for bulk TiO2 rutile and the TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface model. The present study's Hubbard U value for Ti3d was 7.0 eV, which generated an Eg value of around 2.89 eV for the TiO2 surface and agreed with the previously reported value [34].

Moreover, the van der Waals (vdW) interactions also play a leading role in these interactions and contribute significantly to the total energy. When two noncovalently bonded atoms are close enough (within the Bohr exciton limit), the transient dipole in an atom is raised from a brief shift of the orbital electrons to one side of an atom or molecule. Therefore, the induced transient dipole perturbs the electron cloud of the other atom or molecule, causing a similar shift and generating a transient dipole in the second atom. The fluctuating neighbouring dipoles lower their joint energy by correlating their fluctuations, and thus the two dipoles attract each other [35]. It is noteworthy to highlight that the vdW forces represent electrostatic forces characterised by the dipole moment and atomic polarizability. These forces occur in all molecules (polar and nonpolar) involving either transiently or permanently induced electric dipoles [35,36]. The DFT calculation considers the effect of the vdW interaction through an empirical approach based on the Grimme dispersion-corrected DFT-D method [37]. Therefore, we implemented the DFT-D approach and standard PBE functional to describe better the non-bonding weak interactions between the atomic layers.

The stability of the GQD modified TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 interface model was calculated from the adsorption energy, Eads of the GQD molecule adsorbed on the TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface. Eads was estimated from simulated total energy calculations according to the following formula:

are the total energies of the GQD and TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface, respectively. Based on the stable, optimised TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface cell, the interfacial electronic structures, optical properties, and charge transfer characteristics for the TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface model and GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 interface model were calculated.

A green approach was undertaken to synthesise GQD through the hydrolysis of corn powder. Briefly, corn powder was dissolved in 25 ml of deionised (DI) water and stirred for 30 min before being autoclaved for 8 h at 180 °C in stainless steel lined with Teflon. The resulting solution was centrifuged for 15 min at 14,000 RPM to remove the solution's black residue particles to obtain the brown GQD solution.

All employed reagents were of analytical grade and applied without further purification. GQD@TiO2 nanorods were prepared through a hydrothermal process. TiO2 nanorods were prepared by mixing 20 ml of hydrochloric acid-36% with 20 ml of DI water before adding 0.66 ml of Titanium (IV) Butoxide (TBT) as a titania precursor. The solution was stirred for 3 h before being transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. A piece of Fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) (15 Ω/square) glass substrate with a dimension of 4 × 2 cm was immersed in the solution with the conducting side facing downward. The autoclave was then sealed, and the reaction was carried out at 150 °C for 12 h, followed by calcination at 450 °C for 1 h. A piece of TiO2 nanorod stripe was immersed in the GQD solution before being loaded into a 100 ml Teflon-lined autoclave for a 3-hour solvothermal reaction at 110 °C to deposit GQD on the TiO2 surface. After treatment, the autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature, and the samples were washed with DI water and air-dried at room temperature. GQD@TiO2 rutile nanorods array with the vertically aligned pattern were thus grown on the FTO substrate.

XRD technique (Bruker D8 Advance instrument) was used to determine the microstructure of the samples within the range of 20 − 80°. Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Zeiss Supra 55VP) with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was also employed to examine the morphology and microstructure of the samples. The sample's crystallinity and microstructure information were investigated using high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, Zeiss Libra 200) coupled with selected area electron diffraction (SAED) at 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were conducted using thermo-scientific K-Alpha. A standard calibration of copper, silver, and gold under high ultra-vacuum (2 × 10-7 torr) was used to determine the material binding energy distributions. The absorption spectrum and charge transfer recombination rate were investigated by UV–visible (UV–vis) diffuse-reflectance and photoluminescence(PL) spectra, respectively. UV–vis spectrophotometry (Cary100) was calibrated with Spectralon 99% reflectance from wavelength 200 to 800 nm. PL characterisation was completed with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba. LabRam HR Evolution) with an excitation wavelength of 325 nm [16].

Photoelectrochemical studies were conducted with Autolab PGSTAT302N via a three-electrode configuration. The TiO2, GQD, and GQD@TiO2 acted as a working electrode (photoanode), and Ag/AgCl saturated KCl and platinum (Pt) rod were reference and counter electrodes respectively. All the electrodes were immersed in a KOH solution with 5 vol% glycerol as a sacrificial agent, which oxidised faster by the positive holes than the hydroxyl group of water. Therefore, the charge carrier recombination rate reduced and electrons' lifetime increased, positively impacting photocatalytic hydrogen production [38]. A halogen lamp (500 W) with a light intensity of 1 Sun (100 mW/cm2) was used as the light source to irradiate the samples. All electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were taken with an AC amplitude of 10 mV with a frequency range of 0.1 to 100 kHz. The photocurrent density was measured using the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) technique with a scan rate of 20 mV/s and applied potential −1 to 1 V vs. Ag/AgCl. Next, Mott-Schottky (M−S) analysis was performed on the EIS procedure to determine the capacitance in the system under depletion conditions as a function of released potential. The flat band potential (Vfb) was measured by extrapolating the M−S plot on V-axis with C2 = 0. The electron donor density (ND) was determined using the subsequent equation,
N D

where ē is the electronic charge unit (1.6 × 10-19C), m is the slope of M−S plot, ɛo is the permittivity of the free space charge (8.86 × 10-12F/m), and ɛ is dielectric constant for the rutile TiO2 [6].

Bulk TiO2 rutile has a tetragonal geometry structure with
D
4 h
14
-
P 4
2
/mnm space group. The basic building block of a TiO2 rutile structure consists of a titanium atom bonded to its six nearest oxygen neighbours in an octahedral configuration. The adjacent octahedra share one corner along [110] directions stacked with the long axis (altering by 90˚). The stacking of the octahedra results in 3-fold coordinated oxygen atoms for bulk TiO2 rutile structure, as reported previously [39]. The simulated optimized lattice parameters for bulk TiO2 rutile unit cell were a = b = 4.567 Å, c = 2.934 Å, and c/a = 0.642, which agreed well with the reported experimental values of a = b = 4.584 Å, c = 2.953 Å, and c/a = 0.644 [40] and deviated marginally for only − 0.37%, − 0.64%, and − 0.31%, respectively. These data indicated that the implemented computational methodology was appropriate for calculating the TiO2 rutile structural properties. Bulk-terminated 5-layer TiO2 rutile (011) surface slab was then constructed from the optimised TiO2 rutile unit cell with a vacuum space of 20 Å above the slab to remove the impact from the adjacent (011) periodic layers. A layer was classified as (011) plane as it contained both Ti and O atoms, implying a symmetric slabs model for surface modelling, which considered the two sides were equivalent and thus eradicated the disproportionate effect of the bulk.

The computationally generated DFT simulation model for the TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface is shown with side and top views in Fig. 1 (a) and (b), respectively. The designed TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface model is also referred to as the 'brookite (001)-like' model in the literature due to its similarities to the fully optimised brookite TiO2 (001) surface model. The model provides a considerable description of the TiO2 (011) – 2 × 1 surface geometry and valuable insights into molecular-scale surface chemical processes. Gong et al. reported that this model fit the X-ray data well, with the lowest χ2 value (χ2 = 6.73) [41]. In previously reported literature, researchers adopted the same model for DFT calculations to study the photocatalytic characteristics of this inspiring surface [41-44]. Herein, we designed the reconstructed TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface from the bulk-terminated TiO2 rutile (011) surface, derived from the rutile TiO2 unit cell. The surface structure was then relaxed by constricting the bottom 3-layer atoms to their bulk positions while the top two layers were fully relaxed, as displayed in Fig. 1 (a).

The reconstructed surface TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 structure contained two inequivalent surface oxygen two-fold coordinated (O2f) atoms, one at the top and the other was on either side of the hillock structure. Apart from these two types of O2f atoms, three-fold coordinated (O3f) atoms were observed in the valleys, which had bulk-like surroundings. At the top of the surface structure, the ridge five-fold coordinated Titanium (Ti5f) atoms were present in a distorted square pyramidal symmetry instead of the usual octahedral bulk structural order. Another type of exposed Ti5f atoms in the valleys, as shown in Fig. 1 (a), does not extend a dangling bond towards the vacuum. Two different Ti-O surroundings for the ridge Ti5f top atoms and the exposed Ti5f valley atoms are also shown in Fig. 1 (a). For further clarification, Fig. 1(b) shows the top view of the slab model for the sub-surface atoms below the top surface layer. In the two-dimensional (2D) top view, the model illustrates a “ring” structure with undercoordinated surface atoms with Ti and O atoms.

The GQD model consisted of seven fused benzene rings containing 36 atoms with 24 carbon and 12H atoms at the edges, as depicted in Fig. 1 (c). The designed GQD has a circular geometry, with carbon atoms in the x-y plane satisfying x2 + y2 < R2, where R is the GQD radius. Several studies referred to this type of GQD model as GQD 7 because its index is generally determined according to the number of fused benzene rings [45-48]. Moreover, it is also confirmed that the adopted GQD model with 36 atoms is within the range of a QD (10 – 50 atoms) [49]. Initially, the edges of the GQD were saturated with H atoms, followed by optimisation. The optimised GQD structure was employed as the primary structure of the GQD for all computational simulations. The C-C bond extracted from the optimised GQD structure had a length of 1.420 Å, which was in good agreement with previously reported theoretical values [50].

The GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 nanocomposite model was designed with GQD physiosorbed on the TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface slab. Different GQD models with 4, 7, and 10-rings structures were designed on the TiO2 surface slab. The estimated Eads of the 7-rings GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) structure was −1.75 eV, which was lower than the 4-rings (−1.59 eV) and 10-rings (−1.67 eV) structures, indicating that it was the most stable configuration. The unoptimized and optimised structures of the 7-rings GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) model are shown in Fig. 2 (a, b), respectively. The 4-rings and 10-rings GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) models are presented in the supplemental information (Figs. S1 and S2). The GQD interacted with TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 substrate via long-range vdW interactions with an average equilibrium separation distance of 2.83 Å or 0.283 nm. Notably, these noncovalent vdW bonds are only formed when the atoms are relatively close to each other. The designed model of the GQD molecule was separated by only ∼ 0.283 nm, which is still much less than the estimated value of the intralayer exciton Bohr radius (∼1 – 3 nm) [35,51-53]. The layer-separated electrons and holes can still generate strong Coulomb interactions to form bound exciton states at the GQD@TiO2 interface. Coulomb's interactions between two neighbouring transient dipoles caused the interfacial charge transfer through these bound exciton states [54,55]. Moreover, since the vdW dispersion interaction was inherently a quantum mechanical phenomenon, therefore, simple quasi-classical models were employed to estimate the quantum fluctuations caused by the induced transient dipoles.

After geometry optimisation, the GQD structure's geometry turned out to be slightly bent due to the oxygen's greater electronegativity (3.44) than carbon (2.55). The central portion of the GQD unit structure curved downwards towards the surface of the TiO2 structure because of the vdW interactions. Meanwhile, the edges of the structure bend up, suggesting a significant interaction between the central fragment of the GQD and the TiO2 facet. On the other hand, the TiO2 surface did not display any changes in its structure as expected due to its rigid inorganic semiconductor nature, as previously reported [29].

Additionally, the effect of multiple adsorbed (bi-layered and tri-layered) GQD molecules on the TiO2 surface (Figs. S3 and S4) was also examined. The bi-layered GQD modified TiO2 system had Eads of −1.64 eV, while the tri-layered was around −1.48 eV. Both values were smaller than the Eads of the monolayered GQD adsorbed system (−1.75 eV), indicating that the multiple GQD systems were less stable than the monolayered GQD system. It is worth mentioning that the computed Eads of the multiple GQD systems declined as the number of adsorbed molecules increased. It might be due to the vdW interactions decreasing rapidly as the distance between the GQD molecules and TiO2 surface increased. Generally, the vdW interaction was proportional to 1/r6, where “r” is the distance between atoms or molecules in different layers [51,52]. Therefore, the stability of the multiple GQD adsorbed systems was lower than the monolayered GQD adsorbed system.

A better understanding of a photocatalyst's electronic structure is vital to improve visible light absorption, charge separation efficiency, and photocatalytic performance. Fig. 3 (a - c) represents the calculated band structure plots of TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface, GQD, and GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 interface model along with highly symmetric Brillouin zone points, respectively. Fig. 3 (a) indicates that the top of the valence band (VB) and bottom of the CB of TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface lies at the same Γ point to claim a direct bandgap [56]. Furthermore, the calculated bandgap value at this highly symmetric Γ point was 2.89 eV, in very close agreement with the previously estimated theoretical value of ∼ 2.87 eV for the same Hubbard's value of U = 7 eV. It proved that the employed DFT + U approach was suitable for bandgap calculations, and the standard DFT method's shortcomings were eliminated [34].

Fig. 3 (b) reveals the calculated Eg of 2.153 eV for a seven ring GQD model. The modified narrow bandgap of GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 interface model (Fig. 3 (c)) indicated that the VB region's energy levels appeared below the Fermi energy level were compressed in the downward direction along with the low energy states. New impurity energy levels of carbon atoms from GQD were also introduced in the vicinity of the Fermi energy level [57]. Meanwhile, the impurity states appeared in the VB region from −15 eV to 0 eV near the Fermi energy level. The CB also shifted towards the Fermi energy level due to the incorporation of GQD impurity states. The visible light energy absorption range was closely matched by the tiny band gap value of 1.78 eV for GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1surface. The results confirmed the insertion of the new impurity states that increased the overall density of the energy states and shifted the conduction band minima (CBM) towards the Fermi energy level, reducing the Eg . In addition, the bandgap reduction here was due to the bottom of the CB moving towards lower energies rather than the top of the VB shifting, which fitted well with the requirements of narrowing the bandgap without decreasing their photocatalytic activity [58].

Fig. 4 (a) to (c) depict the contribution of the partial density of states (PDOS) to the total density of states (TDOS) for the TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface, GQD, and the GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 interface model, respectively. For TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface (Fig. 4 (a)), the oxygen p-like and titanium d-like orbitals predominantly contribute to the formation of VB and CB, respectively. The Fermi Energy Level (EF) at 0 eV has been chosen as the origin of the energy. The VB comprised hybridised O2p and Ti3d states, while the CB was constructed from Ti3d with weakly hybridised O2p states. The O2p states mainly dominated the top of the VB in the locality at the Fermi level, while the bottom of the CB was dominated by Ti 3d states. The TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface had no surface state in the forbidden gap. The O2p states mainly dominated the top of the VB, while the bottom of the CB was predominantly composed of Ti3d states. Therefore, the electrons can be excited directly from C2p orbitals in the upper VB to the lower CB's Ti3d orbitals.

Fig. 4 (b) illustrates that the VB of GQDs is mainly composed of C2p states with a small contribution from C2s states. Meanwhile, for the CB, it only has a significant contribution from C2p states. The TDOS and PDOS of GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 is depicted in Fig. 4 (c), which shows that by introducing GQD into the TiO2 rutile (011) surface slab, DOS increases significantly below EF in the range from −6 to 0 eV. A comparison with TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface showed that new carbon impurity states were formed at the GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface due to the incorporation of GQD, as encircled by the dashed line in Fig. 4 (c). The C2s and O2s states and a small contribution from the C2p contributed significantly to these lower energy impurity peaks in the energy range between −15 and −7 eV.

Furthermore, for the GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 interface model, the intensities, and shapes of the peaks in the upper VB in the energy range between −6 and 0.3 eV were altered as compared to the TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface. The PDOS plot demonstrated that C2p states were added with O2p and Ti3d states, enhancing the intensity of these peaks. In this energy range (between −6 and 0.3 eV), there was also a minor contribution from O2s and C2s states. The results confirmed that the energy bands near the Fermi level were mainly created by C2p and O2p states, with the Ti3d state's contribution, was insignificant. Moreover, GQD shifted the CB towards the Fermi energy level, reducing the Eg to 1.78 eV, consistent with the band structure diagram in Fig. 3 (c). These changes could be attributed to the introduction of the C2p states and increased state density, which may broaden the CB by increasing the numbers of electronic states to a specified level. The Eg was reduced, improving optical absorption in the visible light region significantly. Besides, the VB's bandwidth was increased by inserting C impurity states, which facilitate its hole mobility [59]. The combination effect of narrowing the bandgap and increasing the charge carrier's mobility can substantially improve the photocatalytic performance of the photocatalyst.

Fig. 5 represents the UV–Vis absorption spectra for the TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface slab and GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 interface model. The spectra demonstrated intrinsic transitions between the unoccupied and occupied energy states [32]. Fig. 5 shows that the TiO2 rutile's Eg restricts it to absorb only ultraviolet radiationin a narrow light range (between 200 and 380 nm) of the solar spectrum, thereby limiting its application range. The GQD modified TiO2 model absorption results were compared to the TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface absorption curve as a benchmark. The UV–vis absorption plot for the designed GQD model (inset in Fig. 5) had a prominent transition absorption peak near the 300 nm wavelength range. Introducing GQD to the TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface model reduced the Eg significantly; thus, the absorption curve was amplified considerably, as seen in Fig. 5.

A noteworthy redshift of the absorption peak to the more extended wavelength region for the GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 interface model was related to the bandgap narrowing and reduction in the electron transition energy [60-63]. As electron transition energy decreased, the optical absorption edge observed an evident redshift with enhanced absorption intensity in the visible light region for GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 compared to the TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 facet, which further implied improvement in its photocatalytic properties. The overall increase in the optical absorption peak intensity for the GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 model indicated that the nanocomposite absorbed more photons, leading to more electron-hole pairs generation. These results demonstrated that anchoring GQDs on the surface of TiO2 (011) rutile 2 × 1 caused a shift in the absorption peak towards a longer wavelength with higher intensity. Therefore, higher photon absorption enhanced the photocatalytic performance significantly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Charge density difference display and Mulliken population analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Plane-wave pseudopotential assisted Mulliken population analysis was executed on the TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 slab model and GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 interface model to identify the exact direction of charge transfer at the interface. The 3D electron density difference map with an isosurface value of 4 × 10-4 e/Å3 for the TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 slab is displayed in Fig. 6 (a). The blue-coloured isosurface represented the electronic charge accumulation region, while the yellow-coloured indicated the space charge depletion region. The blue isosurfaces thus appearing around O atoms represented that O atoms had gained electron density. In contrast, the yellow-coloured isosurfaces that grew around Ti atoms indicated that they were losing electron density.

Moreover, the simulated average Mulliken charge values for Ti atoms were around + 1.30, and for O atoms were around −0.72 for the TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface slab, as depicted in Fig. 6 (c). The average Mulliken population values for O atoms were negative but positive for Ti atoms, indicating that O atoms tend to gain electrons while Ti atoms lose them. It could be owing to the difference in electronegativity between O and Ti atoms (O: 3.44 and Ti: 1.54), which allows O atoms to obtain more electrons than Ti atoms.

The electron density difference map for the GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 interface model was also calculated to examine the charge transfer between GQD and TiO2 surface. Due to vdW interactions, a close yet non-destructive contact had developed at the interface, allowing electrons to transfer between GQD and TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface. As displayed in Fig. 6 (b), it can be seen from the map that the charge redistribution took place predominantly at the interface of the GQD molecule and the TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface. The charge transfer from the GQD to the TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface was significant, but there was essentially little charge transfer between the internal O and Ti atoms. The interfacial electron transfer might be attributed to the spatially terminated atoms of the TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface, which developed the undercoordinated Ti atoms. Thus, the formation of the exceeding numbers of oxygen deficiencies in the form of undercoordinated Ti atoms on TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface led to accelerating the charge transfer between GQD and TiO2 surface [45,46].

Fig. 6 (d) shows Mulliken population analysis for the GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 interface model to quantify the computed charge transfer between the GQD and TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface slab. It is noticeable to see that the top layer O2f atoms (red-coloured atoms encircled by dashed line) in the TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 slab (Fig. 6 (c)) had a Mulliken charge of −0.72, whereas the corresponding O2f atoms in GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 interface model (Fig. 6 (d)) had a charge of −0.78. The ridge Ti5f atom below the O2f atoms at the top layer had a charge of + 1.31 before adsorption of GQD, while the same atom had a Mulliken charge of + 1.34 after GQD was added to the TiO2 surface.

On the other hand, the calculated averaged Mulliken charge value for the C atoms in the GQD model was around + 0.026, which altered to + 0.032 for the GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 interface model. These results showed a significant charge transfer from GQD to the TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 plane at the ground electronic state. Consequently, this charge transfer resulted in substantial hole accumulation at the GQD, while electrons accumulated at the TiO2 surface. Run long reported the same trend of charge transfer from GQD to the rutile TiO2 (110) surface in the GQD@TiO2 (110) interface model. An excellent charge transfer promotes the separation of electron-hole pairs to reduce the charge recombination rate. Furthermore, it facilitates good interface contact by transferring more charges, allowing for deeper interaction between layers. Ultimately, all of these factors excellently signify photocatalytic activity [29].

Experimental studies were used to cross-check and verify the accuracy of the computational results. Fig. 7 displays the XRD patterns of the FTO glass substrate, TiO2, GQD, and GQD@TiO2 samples. FTO peaks appeared at 26.61, 37.91, and 51.69° of 2θ, whereas the XRD pattern of the TiO2 (JCPDS 98–016-8138) illustrated the presence of rutile (011) at 2θ values of 36.24° with d-spacing of 0.24 nm as the prominent peak and other rutile peaks with less intensity, including 121, 002, 031, and 112 planes. The GQDs' XRD pattern showed a broad peak centred at around 24 with a d-spacing of 0.36 nm, corresponding to the (002) plane peak and matching JCPDS. Card No, 75–0444. The XRD pattern of the GQDs@TiO2 sample exhibited the same characteristic diffraction peaks as was observed for pure TiO2 rutile, indicating that the GQDs did not affect the TiO2 rutile crystal structure. The increased intensity of the XRD (011) peak of the GQDs@TiO2 rutile sample corresponds to the post-calcination treatment of the sample due to the improved crystallinity of the TiO2 rutile phase [64]. Furthermore, no distinctive GQDs diffraction signals were detected in the GQD@TiO2 composite, which might be attributed to the limited amount and relatively low diffraction intensity of the GQDs, as reported in previous studies [25,65,66].

Fig. 8 (a) and (b) reveal the top and cross-sectional views of the FESEM images of GQD, TiO2, and GQD@TiO2 samples on the surface of the FTO substrate. Fig. 8 (a) shows the deposited GQD on the surface of the FTO substrate via the hydrothermal method, which presents the aggregation of GQD particles. The top view images in Fig. 8 (b) and (c) verify that TiO2 NRs were formed vertically with an average diameter of 141.7 and 233.4 nm for TiO2 and GQD@TiO2, respectively, with a positive effect on the photocatalytic reaction by adsorbing more electrolyte on the photocatalyst surface. The inset of the cross-sectional images in Fig. 8 (b) and (c) indicate that the length of the rod in GQD@TiO2 is longer than the pure TiO2, suggesting that GQDs were deposited on the surface of TiO2 nanorods. A comparison between Fig. 8 (a) and (c) exhibits that the GQD was decorated on the surface of the TiO2 nanorods with a low agglomeration rate, which can improve its surface area with better photocatalytic activity [2].

The size and morphological features of the GQD, TiO2, and GQD@TiO2 were analysed using TEM and HRTEM imaging and selected area electron diffraction (SAED), as displayed in Fig. 9 (a-h). Fig. 9 (a) illustrates the HRTEM image of the as-prepared GQDs with an average size of 5.61 ± 0.4 nm (n = 90). Fig. 9 (b) presents that the primary particles were distributed between 4 and 6 nm with the narrow-distributed particle size. Therefore, depositing the as-prepared GQDs on the TiO2 surface can significantly enhance its photocatalytic hydrogen production.

Meanwhile, Fig. 9 (c) shows a TEM image of crystalline TiO2 nanorods with d-spacings of 0.248 and 0.23 nm, corresponding to TiO2 nanorods with (011) and (020) planes, as confirmed by the HRTEM image in Fig. 9 (d). The TEM image of GQD@TiO2 (Fig. 9 (e)) confirms the presence of evenly arched GQD on the surface of TiO2. The TEM and HRTEM images of GQD@TiO2 in Fig. 9 (f) and (g), respectively, confirm the presence of GQD with a lattice plane of (002) and d-spacing of 0.36 nm. In the meantime, the TiO2 (011) plane with d-spacing 0.248 nm agreed with TiO2 planes. As seen in Fig. 9 (h), the lattice fringes SAED pattern indicated the presence of TiO2 (011) plane.
The presence of Ti, O, and C peaks can be seen in the XPS survey spectra (Fig. 10 (a)) with binding energies of Ti2p (459.26 eV), O1s (531.31 eV), and C1s (285.93 eV), respectively.

For GQD@TiO2, the C1s peak appeared at 285.93 eV, significantly more intense than pure TiO2, and was consistent with TEM analysis. This pattern could be attributed to the addition of GQDs into the TiO2. Next, Fig. 10 (b) identifies that the prominent deconvoluted C1s peak at 284.93 eV was attributed to hybridised carbon in sp2 form (C = C bond). Peaks at 285.2 and 286.44 eV could be due to the sp3 hybridised carbon and the C–O bond species at higher binding energy tails. Moreover, the Ti–O–C structure was detected at a peak around 288.7 eV, demonstrating the interaction of C atoms from GQD with the TiO2 lattice.

Subsequently, Fig. 10 (c) displays that the Ti2p signal can be deconvoluted into two peaks (458.75 and 464.49 eV) for pure TiO2 before shifting to 458.91 and 464.58 eV in GQD@TiO2 composite, respectively. The XPS peak of O1s (Fig. 10 (d)) for pure TiO2 was deconvoluted into the main contribution of lattice oxygen (Ti-O) at 530.03 eV, adsorbed oxygen at 532.58 eV, and the broad peak of adsorbed H2O at 534.16 eV. Meanwhile, the XPS peaks of O2p for Ti-O and adsorbed oxygen shifted to 531.77 and 532.81 eV in GQD@TiO2 composite, respectively. The positive shift of the Ti2p and O1s binding energy was attributed to the presence of C on the composite surface and its interaction with Ti and O, as confirmed by the presence of a Ti–O–C peak [67].

Fig. 11 (a) and (b) display the optical absorption photoluminescence (PL) spectra of TiO2 and GQD@TiO2 nanorods, respectively. Fig. 11 (a) shows that the absorption band edge of the TiO2 rutile nanorods sample was 416 nm due to electron excitations from VB to CB of TiO2 [68]. Due to the aromatic sp2 domains, the plotted UV–vis absorption spectrum for GQDs (inset of Fig. 11 (a) displayed a typical π-π* transition absorption peak around 220 nm, with n-π* transition absorption peak at 345 nm. By introducing GQDs onto TiO2 nanorods, the spectrum had a noticeable red shift in the absorption edge towards 405 nm. Due to the broad absorption ability of GQD@TiO2 that extends to 700 nm, the photo-response was further extended to visible light and the near-infrared (NIR) range. The PL spectra for the prepared photocatalysts were then depicted in Fig. 11 (b), which describe the recombination dynamics for the excited electron-hole pairs.

The pure TiO2 sample had a broad and intense PL band than the GQD modified TiO2 due to the fast charge recombination rate. Broadband presented at 450–550 nm was due to the recombination of the photogenerated electrons-holes due to surface states and self-trapped excites on the rutile TiO2 phase [69]. A shoulder around 600–650 nm also appeared for the oxygen vacancies in the active phase [70-74]. Since GQD@TiO2 displayed less broad and intense PL peaks than pure TiO2, the charge carrier's recombination rate was decreased sufficiently. The presence of photogenerated electrons and holes at the electrode/electrolyte interface was enhanced by decreasing the recombination rate, which accelerated the photocatalytic activity [75].

The Nyquist plots for TiO2 and GQD@TiO2 rutile are displayed in Fig. 12 (a) and (b). The proper equivalent circuit for TiO2 and GQD@TiO2 were R(RQ) and [R(Q(RW))], respectively. Table 1 represents the summarised fitting results. The RQ corresponds to higher frequencies' electron transfer resistance and recombination tendency at the photoanode/electrolyte interface [76-78]. Rs stands for sheet resistance, Rct for charge transfer resistance, and CPEPh denotes photoanode double-layer capacitance.

GQD@TiO2 nanorods had smaller semicircle radii than TiO2 due to lower Rct (248.33 Ω) and longer electron lifetime τn (160.44 ms) as compared to pure TiO2 nanorods with Rct (7040 Ω) and τn (6.24 ms). These results suggested that frequent charge transfer facilitated electron-hole pair separation and reduced recombination rates. The absolute position of the semiconductor's band-edge potential is one of the most crucial characteristics that play a prominent role in photocatalytic activity. The Mott-Schottky plot (M−S) for the synthesised TiO2 and GQD@TiO2 nanorods are shown in Fig. 12 (c), while Table 1 shows the calculated values for the ND and Vfb obtained from M−S plots.

Furthermore, the M−S plots for the as-prepared pure TiO2 and GQD@TiO2 nanorods photocatalysts had positive slopes, confirming the nature of n-type semiconductors with electrons as the primary charge carriers. The GQD@TiO2 nanorods sample's Vfb was shifted to a more negative potential with a higher ND than pure TiO2, indicating a significant accumulation of electrons at the photocatalyst's surface. The more negative Vfb for an n-type semiconductor, the more remarkable the capacity to convert water to hydrogen in a photoelectrochemical cell. Moreover, compared to TiO2 photocatalysts, the GQD@TiO2 had a higher ND, resulting in better electrical conductivity and more charge carriers at the electrode/electrolyte interface [14,79].

On the other hand, Fig. 12 (d) plots the photocurrent density of the prepared photoanodes under visible light irradiation in the range of −0.7 to 1 V bias voltage. Due to photoinduced emerging electron accumulation at GQDs, the photocurrent density value of the GQD@TiO2 was higher than the pure TiO2 at the same bias voltage, positively affecting photoconversion efficiency and hydrogen production rate [80,81]. The prepared photoanodes were investigated for hydrogen production by immersing them in a KOH and glycerol solution before connecting them to a dye solar cell to provide the bias voltage of 0.6 V. hydrogen was produced around the Pt counter electrode before being collected in the reverse burette. Based on Fig. 12 (e), the GQD@TiO2 photoanode demonstrated the highest hydrogen production yield. However, in the absence of GQD, the TiO2 photoanode showed a significant drop after two hours. The hydrogen production rate over the GQD@TiO2 photoanode was 31063 µmol g-1h−1, five times greater than the pure TiO2 photoanode (6931 µmol g-1h−1).

Fig. 13 represents a schematic illustration of the interfacial charge transfer on the surface of GQDs@TiO2 hybrid photocatalyst for hydrogen production with the glycerol oxidation process under 500 W halogen light irradiation. The XPS VB spectra examined the band structure of GQD, TiO2, and GQD@TiO2 nanorods and are given in Fig. S5. The calculated VB potential (EVB) for GQD and pure TiO2 GQD deposited on TiO2 nanorods were at 2, 2.8, and 2.5 eV, respectively. Since the work function of the XPS instrument was 4.62 eV, therefore, the calculated EVB for GQD, pure TiO2, and GQD decorated on the surface of TiO2 nanorods were 2.12, 2.92, and 2.62 eV (vs. standard hydrogen electrode (SHE), provided 4.5 eV vs. vacuum level was 0 V vs. SHE), respectively. The CB energy (ECB) was calculated using the following equation:
E CB

The Eg of GQD and TiO2 nanorods was 2.6 and 2.99 eV, respectively, as presented in Fig. S6. GQD and TiO2 nanorods had ECB values of −0.45 and −0.07, respectively [82-84]. In this study, GQD served as a photosensitiser and solid-state electronic transfer reagent to enhance photocatalytic hydrogen production significantly [21,26,85]. Under the irradiation of light, GQDs deposited on the TiO2 surface acted as electron reservoirs and solid-state electronic transfer reagents to trap photoinduced electrons from CB of TiO2 and efficiently separate electrons and holes to enhance photocatalytic hydrogen evolution. Moreover, the π conjugated GQDs behaved like photosensitisers when excited by light photons (as an organic dye sensitise TiO2), resulting in photo-excited electrons migrating from GQDs to TiO2 CB and improving hydrogen production. Electrochemical studies and optical properties confirmed that depositing GQDs on the surface of TiO2 nanorods enhanced its light absorption in the visible range, increased electron-hole separation and charge transfers, expanded electron lifetime, and shifted the Vfb to a more negative potential [86].

Table 2 compares the photocatalytic hydrogen production performance of the synthesised GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) photocatalyst to that of various previously published studies. Comparing the performance of this photocatalyst to previous results was difficult due to the different reaction conditions and TiO2 crystal structures and shapes. Hence, we scaled up the data for 1 g of photocatalyst, and the hydrogen production unit was fixed to µmol. It should be noticed that the 1D TiO2 (011) rutile thin-film anchored with GQDs produced more hydrogen in the visible region than the others. The improved performance could be attributed to 1D TiO2 nanorods with better charge transfer properties and (011) plane of the TiO2 with higher photocatalytic reactivity than other phases.

Furthermore, the PEC cell was connected to another photovoltaic cell to overcome the limitations of a single photoelectrochemical cell by providing sufficient potential for water reduction to hydrogen. Additionally, we conducted the electrochemical experiments in the PEC cell, which diminished the drawbacks of the backward reaction between hydrogen and oxygen and photocatalyst agglomeration compared to the batch reactor system [87].

The synthesised GQDs were deposited on the less-investigated TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface using a green and less toxic method to improve the solar hydrogen production in the PEC cell. Both computational and experimental studies were conducted to understand the effect of GQD on the electronic structure, optical properties, and charge transfer characteristics of the TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface. The DFT calculation confirmed that the addition of GQDs on the TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 surface model significantly reduced Eg and enhanced absorption intensity with a redshift for the optical absorption edge. Many charges were transferred from GQD to the TiO2 surface, reducing the recombination rate presented by the Mulliken population analysis and charge density difference display. The experimental results for the designed GQD@TiO2 rutile (011) – 2 × 1 hybrid photocatalyst were in good agreement with the theoretical outcomes. This study revealed that the hydrogen production rate for the hybrid photocatalyst was about five times higher than that of pristine TiO2, implying that the photocatalytic performance was improved remarkably. These results also provided valuable guidance and new insights for further research into the 1D TiO2 rutile (011) phase, which has photocatalytic activity potential, and its modification with GQD via a less toxic approach.
